stem: Rank the following four functions by growth rate as \( n\to\infty \): \( n \), \( n\log n \), \( n^{[[k]]} \), and \( [[c]]^{n} \). The fastest-growing is \( [[c]]^{n} \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{n^{[[k]]}}{[[c]]^{n}} \), the ratio of the SECOND-fastest-growing function to the fastest.
solution: Among the four, \( n \) and \( n\log n \) both lose to any positive power of \( n \), so the second-fastest is \( n^{[[k]]} \). Since exponentials dominate polynomials, \( \frac{n^{[[k]]}}{[[c]]^n}\to [[answer]] \) as \( n\to\infty \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the exponent and base as if they combined into a simple ratio.
This is the reciprocal of the previous incorrect ratio.
This assumes the second-fastest and fastest functions grow at comparable rates.
This evaluates an unrelated power instead of the limit of the ratio.
This subtracts the base from the exponent instead of taking the limit.
This subtracts the exponent from the base instead of taking the limit.

stem: Four power functions have distinct positive integer exponents: \( n^{[[a1]]} \), \( n^{[[a2]]} \), \( n^{[[a3]]} \), and \( n^{[[a4]]} \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{n^{[[a3]]}}{n^{[[a4]]}} \), the ratio of the SECOND-fastest-growing of the four to the fastest.
solution: The largest exponent, [[a4]], makes \( n^{[[a4]]} \) the fastest-growing; the next-largest, [[a3]], makes \( n^{[[a3]]} \) second-fastest. Since \( [[a3]] < [[a4]] \), \( \frac{n^{[[a3]]}}{n^{[[a4]]}} = n^{[[a3]]-[[a4]]}\to [[answer]] \) as \( n\to\infty \).
distractor_rationales: This is the ratio of the exponents themselves, not the limit of the function ratio.
This is the reciprocal of the exponent ratio, still not the limit.
This assumes the second-fastest and fastest functions grow at the same rate.
This is the positive exponent difference, not the value of the limit.
This is the negative exponent difference used incorrectly as if it were the answer directly.
This guesses a generic negative value instead of computing the actual limit.

stem: Consider the three functions \( n^{[[a]]} \), \( [[c]]^{n} \), and \( [[b]]^{n} \). Both exponentials outgrow the polynomial, and \( [[b]]^n \) is the fastest-growing of all three. Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{[[c]]^{n}}{[[b]]^{n}} \), the ratio of the SECOND-fastest-growing function to the fastest.
solution: Since \( [[c]] < [[b]] \), the ratio \( \left(\frac{[[c]]}{[[b]]}\right)^n \to 0 \) as \( n\to\infty \); the polynomial \( n^{[[a]]} \) never even enters this comparison since both exponentials already dominate it.
distractor_rationales: This is the ratio of the bases themselves, mistaken for the limit.
This is the reciprocal of the base ratio, still not the limit.
This assumes the two exponentials grow at the same rate.
This is the positive base difference rather than the value of the limit.
This mixes in the unrelated polynomial exponent instead of comparing the two exponential bases.
This is the negative base difference rather than the value of the limit.
